text: In this video we'll be discussing a laparoscopic appendectomy for perforated appendicitis.
The laparoscopic camera provides a view into the right lower quadrant, revealing free fluid which is suctioned out using a surgical suction device.
Dense adhesions are visible between the appendix, large bowel, small bowel, and reproductive organs, including the ovary and fallopian tubes.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to break up dense adhesions surrounding the appendix, allowing for better visualization of its full extent.
The surgeon gently manipulates the inflamed bowel to avoid injury while continuing the laparoscopic appendectomy for perforated appendicitis.
The surgeon enters an abscess in the right lower quadrant during the laparoscopic appendectomy for perforated appendicitis.
The surgeon uses suction to remove pus from the abdominal cavity, aiming to minimize contamination during the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator as a blunt dissecting tool to separate tissues, focusing on the dense adhesions between the appendix, large bowel, small bowel, and reproductive organs.
The surgeon carefully manipulates inflamed tissue, occasionally using grasping techniques due to the challenging nature of the case, while avoiding unnecessary injury.
The surgeon follows the appendix down to the cecum, deviating from the usual method of tracing the tinea to their convergence on the appendix.
The surgeon continues the dissection and identifies the area of perforation in the appendix, which is highlighted in a freeze frame.
The surgeon mobilizes the right colon, a technique often used in challenging appendectomy cases to improve access and visibility of the appendix and surrounding structures.
The surgeon dissects along the white line of Toldt to medialize the right colon, enhancing visibility and access to the appendiceal base while minimizing the risk of ureteral injuries.
The surgeon uses an energy device as a dissecting instrument to enhance the efficiency of the appendectomy procedure, carefully manipulating tissue with precision.
The surgeon lifts the appendix to tent up the cecum, creating the critical view of safety. This maneuver allows for evaluation of the distance between the appendiceal base and the terminal ileum.
The surgeon evaluates the adequacy of the appendectomy by ensuring no retained appendiceal base is visible, preventing stump appendicitis and occlusion at the terminal ileum.
The surgeon takes small bites with the dissecting instrument, carefully continuing the dissection around the appendix to avoid injuring the surrounding bowel.
The surgeon reaches the appendiceal base and uses a dissecting instrument to create a window between the appendiceal base and the mesoappendix, carefully spreading the tissue.
The surgeon uses a technique involving sweeping up with one hand while pushing down with the other to facilitate blunt dissection, enlarging the window at the appendiceal base and mesoappendix.
The surgeon uses an energy device to safely resect the remaining portion of the mesoappendix, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon achieves a critical view with the appendix elevated, tenting the cecum, and providing a clear view of the terminal ileum.
The surgeon prepares to staple the appendiceal base using a stapler with a one millimeter closed staple height, aiming to minimize bleeding at the staple line while ensuring a secure closure.
The surgeon assesses the appendiceal base near the cecum for perforation, considering the placement of the staple line on the cecum to ensure a secure closure.
The surgeon inspects the staple line, ensuring it is intact and avoiding firing staples across inflamed or unhealthy tissue.
The surgical field shows effective hemostasis, with no active bleeding visible after the meso-appendix has been taken using an energy device.
The surgeon irrigates the right lower quadrant and pelvis, ensuring cleanliness of the surgical field. The omentum is then placed over the staple line for protection.
The surgeon places the appendix into an endoscopic retrieval bag and removes it from the patient's abdomen, marking the completion of the appendectomy procedure.
